event.desc: Premiered at Theatre Royal, Covent Garden 8 Feb. 1800

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible

titlePage.byline: by THOMAS MORTON. Author of A Cure for the Heart Ache, Town and Country, Way to get Married, School of Reform, Secrets worth Knowing, Education, Knight of Snowdown, Zorinski, Columbus, Children in the Wood, Roland for an Oliver, School for Grown Children, Slave, Invincibles, &c.
titlePage.docImprint: Thomas Hailes Lacy, 89, Strand, (Oppositre Southampton Street, Covent Garden Market,) London

performance.p: First performed at the Theatre Royal, Covent Garden, on February 8th, 1800

div.head: Costumes, - Period, about 1796.
div.p: Sir Philip. — Black velvet coat and breeches, white satin waist coat, silk stockings, shoes, buckles, powdered hair, ruffles, and cocked hat.
Morrington. — A dark suit, boots, round hat, and large cloak.
Sir Abel Handy.— Court suit, white silk stockings, shoes and buckles, powdered wig, three-cornered hat, long white lace cravat, ruffles, and cane.
Bob Handy. — Body coat, bright buttons, white waistcoat, white breeches, silk stockings, buckles.
Farmer Ashfield. — Light coat, red waistcoat, corduroy breeches, worsted stockings, shoes, buckles, and round hat.
Henry. — Shooting coat, pouch, shot belt, drab breeches, gaiters.
Evergreen.— Light brown coat, coloured waistcoat, drab breeches, blue stockings, gardener's apron, one corner turned up, and countryman's round hat.
Gerald. — Brown suit, dark grey coat, and boots.
Postillion. — Red jacket, with white buttons, yellow breeches, and top boots.
John. — Smart livery coat, waistcoat, and breeches, white stock ings, and shoes.
Miss Blandford. — Handsome white muslin dress, trimmed with white ribbon, hat, with white feathers, white kid gloves, white shoes, and scarf.
Lady Handy. — An extravagant silk dress, satin shoes, hat and feathers.
Susan.— Neat flowered muslin dress, black shoes, light straw or chip hat, trimmed with white ribbon.
Dame. — Light blue quilted petticoat, neat chintz gown, white stockings, black shoes and buckles, white apron, muslin cap and hankerchief, black mittens, red cloak, and black silk bonnet.

div.stage: SCENE I. —The Entrance to the Castle—lodge and gates, R. —a white rustic gate, L. 2 E., leading to Ashfield’s farm- house, which is seen in part.
Farmer Ashfield discovered seated on a wooden stool, with his pipe, a jug, &c. on a table by him. Enter Dame Ashfield in a cloak and hat, and a basket under her arm, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, Dame, welcome whoam. What news does thee bring vrom market?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: What news, husband? What I always told you; that Farmer Grundy's wheat brought five shillings a quarter more than ours did.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: All the better vor he.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Ah! the sun seems to shine on purpose for him.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Come, come, missus, as thee hast not the grace to thank God for prosperous times, dan't thee grumble when they be unkindly a bit.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: And I assure you, Dame Grundy's butter was quite the crack of the market.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Be quiet, woolye? aleways ding, dinging Dame Grundy into my ears—what will Mrs. Grundy zay? What will Mrs. Grundy think—Canst thee be quiet, let ur alone, and behave thyzel pratty?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: —Certainly I can—I'll tell thee, Tummus, what she said at church last Sunday.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Canst thee tell what parson zaid? Noa—Then I'll tell thee—A' zaid that envy were as foul a weed as grows, and cankers all wholesome plants that be near it—that's what a' zaid.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: And do you think I envy Mrs. Grundy indeed?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Why dant thee letten her aloane then—I do verily think when thee goest to t'other world, the vurst question thee ax 'il; be, if Mrs. Grundy's there—Zoa be quiet, and behave pratty, do'e — Has thee brought whoam the "Salisbury News."

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: No, Tummus: but I have brought a rare wadget of news with me. First and foremost I saw such a mort of coaches, servants, and waggons, all belonging to Sir Abel Handy, and all coming to the castle ; and a handsome young man, dressed all in lace, pulled off his hat to me, and said—"Mrs. Ashfield, do me the honour of presenting that letter to your husband."—So there he stood without his hat—Oh, Tummus, had you seen how Mrs. Grundy looked!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Dom Mrs. Grundy—be quiet, and let I read, woolye? (reads) "My dear farmer," (taking off his hat) Thankye zur—zame to you, wi' all my heart and soul—"My dear Farmer"—

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Farmer—Why, thee're blind, Tummus, it is—"My dear Feyther"—Tis from our own dear Susan.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Odds dickens and daizies! zoo it be, zure enow!—"My dear Feyther, you will be surprized"—Zoo I be, he, he! What pretty writing, bean't it? all as straight as thof it were ploughed—"surprised to hear, that in a few hours I shall embrace you—Nelly, who was formerly our servant, has fortunately married Sir Abel Handy, Bart."—

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Handy Bart.—pugh! Bart. stands for Baronight, mun.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Likely, likely,—Drabbit it, only to think of the zwaps and changes of this world!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Our Nelly married to a great Baronet! I wonder, Tummus, what Mrs. Grundy will say?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Now, woolye be quiet, and let I read—"And she has proposed bringing me to see you; an offer, I hope, as acceptable to my dear feyther"—

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: "And mother"—

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Bless her, how prettily she do write "feyther", dan't she?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: And mother.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p:
Ees, but feyther first, though——"As acceptable to my dear feyther and mother, as to their affectionate
daughter—Susan Ashfield."—Now bean't that a pratty letter?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: And, Tummus, is not she a pretty girl?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees; and as good as she be pratty—Drabbit it, I do feel zoo happy, and zoo warm,—for all the world like the zun in harvest.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Oh, Tummus, I shall be so pleased to see her, I shan't know whether I stand on my head or my heels.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Stand on thy head! vor sheame o' thyzel—behave pratty, do.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Nay, I meant no harm—Eh, here comes friend Evergreen, the gardener, from the castle. Bless me, what a hurry the old man be in.

div.stage: Enter Evergreen, L.U.E.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Good day, honest Thomas.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Zame to you, measter Evergreen.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Have you heard the news?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Any thing about Mrs. Grundy?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Dame, be quiet, woolye now?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: No, no: the news is, that my master, Sir Philip Blandford, after having been abroad for twenty years, returns this day to the castle; and that the reason of his coming is, to marry his only daughter to the son of Sir Abel Handy, I think they call him.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: As sure as tuppence, that is Nelly's husband.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Indeed!—Well, Sir Abel and his son will be here immediately; and, Farmer, you must attend them.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Likely, likely.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: And, mistress, come and lend us a hand at the castle, will you?—Ah, twenty long years since I have seen Sir Philip—Poor gentleman! Bad, bad health—worn almost to the grave, I am told.—-What a lad do I remember him—till that dreadful—(checking himself) But where is Henry? I must see him—must caution him—(a gun is discharged at a distance) That's his gun, I suppose ; he is not far then—Poor Henry!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p:
Poor Henry! I like that indeed! What though he be nobody knows who, there is not a girl in the parish that is not ready to pull caps for him. The Miss Grundys, genteel as they think themselves, would
be glad to snap at him. If he were our own, we could not love him better.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: And he deserves to be loved. Why, he's as handsome as a peach tree in blossom; and his mind is as free from weeds as my favourite carnation bed. But, Thomas, run to the castle, and receive Sir Abel and his son.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I wool, I wool—Zo, good day. (bowing) Let every man make his bow, and behave pratty—that's what I say.—Missus, do'ye show un Sue's letter, woolye? Do ye letten see how pratty she do write feyther.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: But, come step in, and I'll tell you such a story about Mrs. Grundy. You must needs be weary; and I am sure a mug of harvest beer, sweetened with a hearty welcome, will refresh thee. Exeunt into the house, L.

div.stage: Servants cross the stage, L. to R., laden with different packages.— Two Servants enter, L., with the Plough — they exeunt through the gates, R.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Drabbit it, the wold castle 'ull be hardly big enow to hold all thie lumber. Who be this? A do zeem, a comical zoart of a man. Oh, Abel Handy, I suppose.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (without, L.U.E.) Gently there! mind how you go, Robin. (a crash, L.)

Zounds and fury! you have killed the whole county, you dog! for you have broke the patent medicine chest, that was to keep them all alive! (calls off) Richard, gently! Take care of the grand Archimedian corkscrews! Bless my soul! so much to think of! Such wonderful inventions in conception, in concoction, and in completion! Enter Peter, L.U.E. Well, Peter, is the carriage much broke?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Smashed all to pieces. I thought as how, sir, that your infallible axletree would give way.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Confound it! and I declare my water-proof shoes are completely soaked through. Exit Peter, at gates, R.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (loud and bluntly, R.) Zarvent, zur! Zarvent!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (L.C. starting) What's that? Oh, good day.—Devil take the fellow!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Thankye, zur; zame to you wi' all my heart and zoul.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Pray, friend, could you contrive gently to inform me, where I can find one Farmer Ashfield.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ha, ha, ha! (laughing loudly) Excuse my tittering a bit—but your axing mysel vor I be so precious zilly (bowing and laughing)—Ah! you stare at I becase I be bashful and daunted.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: You are very bashful, to be sure. I declare I'm quite weary.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: If you'll walk into the castle, you may zit down, I dare zay.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: May I indeed? you are a fellow of extraordinary civility.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: There's no denying it, zur.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No, I'll sit here.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: What! on the ground! Why you'll wring your ould withers—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: On the ground—no, I always carry my seat with me (spreads a small camp chair)—Here I'll sit and examine the surveyor's account of the castle.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Dickens and daizeys! what a gentleman you would be to shew at a vair!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Silence fellow, and attend—"An account of the castle and domain of Sir Philip Blandford, intended to be settled as a marriage portion on his daughter, and the son of Sir Abel Handy,—by Frank Flourish, surveyor.—Imprimis—The premises command an exquisite view of the Isle of Wight."—Charming! delightful! I don't see it though (rising)—I'll try with my new glass—my own invention—(he looks through the glass, Ashfield peeping through the other end) Yes, there I caught it—Ah! now I see it plainly—Eh! no—I don't see it, do you?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Noa, zur, I doant—but little zweepy do tell I he can zee a bit out from the top of the chimbley—zoa, an you've a mind to crawl up you may zee un too, he, he!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Thank you—but curse your titter. (reads)—"Fish ponds well stocked"—that's a good thing, Farmer.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Likely, likely—but I doant think the vishes do thrive much in theas ponds.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No! why?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Why, the ponds be always dry i'the zummer; and I be tould that bean't wholesome vor the little vishes.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Not very, I believe—Well said surveyor! "A cool summer house."

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur, quite cool—by reason the roof be tumbled in.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Better and better—"the whole capable of the greatest improvement."—Come, that seems true however—I shall have plenty to do, that's one comfort—I have such contrivances! I'll have a canal run through my kitchen. (aside) I must give this rustic some idea of my consequence. You must know, Farmer, you have the honour of conversing with a man, who has obtained patents for tweezers, tooth-picks, and tinder boxes—to a philosopher,who has now in hand two inventions which will render him immortal—the one is, converting saw dust into deal boards, and the other is, a plan of cleaning rooms by a steam engine—and, Farmer, I mean to give prizes for industry—I'll have a ploughing match.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Will you, zur?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes; for I consider a healthy young man, between the handles of a plough, as one of the noblest illustrations of the prosperity of Britain.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Faith and troth! there be some tightish hands in theas parts, I promize ye.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: And, Farmer, it shall precede the hymeneal festivities—

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Nan!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Blockhead! The ploughing match shall take place as soon as Sir Philip Blandford and his daughter arrive.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Oh, likely, likely.

div.stage: Enter John, L.U.E

sp.speaker: John.
sp.p: Sir Abel, I beg to say, my master will be here immediately.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Indeed! and where is Bob?

sp.speaker: John.
sp.p: Why, Sir, the natives were ringing a peal in honour of our arrival—when my master, finding they knew nothing of the matter, went up to the steeple to instruct them. Will you allow me to take this chair in for you? He takes the camp chair and exit at gates, R.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Wonderful! My Bob, you must know, is an astonishing fellow!—you have heard of the admirable Crichton, maybe? Bob's of the same kidney! I contrive, he executes—Sir Abel invenit, Bob fecit. He can do everything—everything!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: All the better vor he. I zay, zur, as he can turn his head to everything, pray, in what way med he earn his livelihood?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Earn his livelihood!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur;—How do he gain his bread!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Bread! Oh, he can't earn his bread, bless you! he's a genius.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Genius! Drabbit it, I have got a horze o' thic name, but dom' un, he'll never work—never.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Egad; here comes my boy Bob!—Eh! no—it is not! no.

div.stage: Enter Bob Handy, with a Postboy's cap and whip, L.U.E. followed by Postboy, with a round hat and cane, L.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Ah, my old Dad, is that you?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Certainly! the only doubt is, if that be you?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Oh, I was teaching this fellow to drive—Nothing is so horrible as people pretending to do what they are unequal to—Give me my hat—That's the way to use a whip.(gives the Postboy his cap and whip)

sp.speaker: Postb.
sp.p: Sir, you know you have broke the horses' knees all to pieces.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Hush, there's a guinea.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (to Ashfield.) You see, Bob can do everything. But, sir, when you knew I had arrived from Germany, why did you not pay your duty to me in London?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (L.)Sir, I heard you were but four days married, and I would not interrupt your honeymoon.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Four days! oh, you might have come.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I hear you have taken to your arms a simple rustic, unsophisticated by fashionable follies—a full blown blossom of nature.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: How does it answer?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: So, so!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Any thorns?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: A few.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I must be introduced—where is she?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Not within thirty miles; for I don't hear her.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (R.)Ha, ha, ha!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Who is that?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (C.)Oh, a pretty behaved tittering friend of mine.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Zarvent, zur—No offence, I do hope—Could not help tittering a bit at Nelly—when she were zarvent maid wi' I, she had a tightish prattle wi' her, that's vor zartain.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Oh! so then my honoured mamma was the servant of this tittering gentleman—I say, father, perhaps she has not lost the tightish prattle he speaks of.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: My dear boy, come here—Prattle! I say did you ever live next door to a pewterer's?—that's all—you understand me—did you ever hear a dozen fire-engines full gallop?—were you ever at Billingsgate in the sprat season?—or——

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Ha, ha!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Nay, don't laugh, Bob.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Indeed, sir, you think of it too seriously. The storm, I dare say, soon blows over.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Soon! you know what a trade wind is, don't you, Bob? why, she thinks no more of the latter end of her speech, than she does of the latter end of her life—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Ha, ha?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: But I won't be laugh'd at—I'll knock any man down that laughs! Bob, if you can say any thing pleasant, I'll trouble you; if not, do what my wife can't—hold your tongue.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I'll shew you what I can do—I'll amuse you with this native.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Do—do—quiz him—at him, Bob.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (crosses to C.)I say, Farmer, you are a set of jolly fellows here, an't you?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (R.)Ees, zur, deadly jolly—excepting when we be otherwise, and then we bean't.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (C.)Play at cricket, don't you?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur; we Hampshire lads conceat we can bowl a bit, or thereabouts.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: And cudgel too, I suppose?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: At him, Bob.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur, we sometimes break oon another's heads, by way of being agreeable, and the like o'that.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Understand all the guards? (putting himself in an attitude of cudgelling.)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Can't zay I do, zur.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: What! hit in this way, eh? (makes a hit at Ashfield, which he parries)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Noa, zur, we do hit thic way. (hits Bob violently)

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Zounds and fury! (crosses L.)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (C.) Why, Bob, he has broke your head.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (L.)Yes; he rather hit me—he somehow——

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: He did, indeed, Bob.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Hang him! The fact is, I am out of practice.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (R.)You need not be, zur; I'll gi' ye a belly full any day, wi' all my heart and soul.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No, no, thank you—Farmer, what's your name?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (R.) My name be Tummus Ashfield—any thing to say against my name?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No, no—Ashfield! should he be the father of my pretty Susan—Pray have you a daughter?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, I have—any thing to zay against she?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No, no; I think her a charming creature.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Do ye, faith and troth—Come, that be deadly kind o'ye however—Do you zee, I were frightful she were not agreeable.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Oh, she's extremely agreeable to me, I assure you.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I vow, it be quite pratty in you to take notice of Sue. I do hope, zur, breaking your head will break noa squares—She be a coming down to theas parts wi' lady, our maid Nelly, as wur—your spouse, zur.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: The devil she is! that's awkward!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I do hope you'll be kind to Sue when she do come, woolye, zur?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: You may depend on it.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I dare say you may. Come, Farmer, I wish to find Miss Blandford — attend us.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur; wi' all respect—Gentlemen, please to walk this way, and I'll walk before you.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Now, that's what he calls behaving pratty.
sp.stage: Exeunt Ashfield, followed by Sir Abel and Bob, through gates, R.

div.stage: Enter Morrington, L.U.E., meeting Gerald, R.U.E

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: (L.)Well, Gerald, my trusty fellow, is Sir Philip arrived?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: No, sir; but hourly expected.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Tell me, how does the castle look?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: (R.)Sadly decayed, sir.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: I hope, Gerald, you were not observed.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: I fear otherwise, sir; on the skirts of the domain I encountered a stripling with his gun; but I darted into that thicket, and so avoided him.

div.stage: Henry appears, L.U.E., in a shooting dress, attentively observing them.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Have you gained any intelligence?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: None: the report that reached us was false—The infant certainly died with its mother—Hush! conceal yourself—we are observed—this way.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: (advancing)Hold! as a friend, one word! (he follows them, and returns, R.1.E.) Again they have escaped me—"The infant died with its mother"—This agony of doubt is insupportable.

div.stage: Enter Evergreen from house,L.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Henry, well met.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Have you seen strangers?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: No!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Two but now have left this place—They spoke of a lost child—My busy fancy led me to think I was the object of their search—I pressed forward, but they avoided me.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: No, no; it could not be you; for no one on earth knows but myself, and——

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Who? Sir Philip Blandford?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: I am sworn, you know, my dear boy; I am solemnly sworn to silence.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p:
True, my good old friend; and if the knowledge of who I am can only be obtained at the price of thy
perjury, let me for ever remain ignorant—let the corroding thought still haunt my pillow, cross me at every turn, and render me insensible to the blessings of health and liberty. Yet it is hard to think, that the most detested reptile that nature forms, or man pursues, has, when he gains his den, a parent's pitying breast to shelter in; but I——

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Come, come, no more of this.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Well!——I visited to-day that young man who was so grievously bruised by the breaking of his team.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: That was kindly done, Henry.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: I found him suffering under extreme torture; yet a ray of joy shot from his languid eye—for his medicine was administered by a father's hand—it was a mother's precious tear that dropped upon his wound—Oh, how I envied him!

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Still on the same subject—I tell thee, if thou art not acknowledged by thy race, why, then become the noble founder of a new one.—Come with me to the castle, for the last time. (crosses R.)

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: The last time!

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Aye, boy; for, when Sir Philip arrives, you must avoid him.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Not see him! where exists the power that shall prevent me?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Henry, if you value your own peace of mind—if you value an old man's comfort, avoid the castle.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Well, I am content.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: That's right—that's right, Henry; Be thou but resigned and virtuous, and he who clothes the lily of the field, will be a parent to thee.

div.stage: Enter Dame from house, L, with lace on a cushion, she sits and commences work, L. Enter Bob Handy at gates, R.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p:
A singular situation this my old dad has placed me in; brought me here to marry a woman of fashion and beauty, while I have been professing, and, I've a notion feeling, the most ardent love for the pretty Susan Ashfield—Propriety says, take Miss Blandford—Love says, take Susan—Fashion says, take both—but would Susan consent to such an arrangement?—and if she
refused, would I consent to part with her?—Oh, time enough to put that question, when the previous one is disposed of—(seeing Dame) How do you do? How do you do?—Making lace, I perceive—Is it a common employment, here?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Oh, no, sir; nobody can make it in these parts but myself. Mrs. Grundy, indeed, pretends; but, poor woman! she knows no more of it than you do.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Than I do! that's vastly well;—My dear madam, I passed two months at Mechlin https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mechlin_lacefor the express purpose.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Indeed!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: You don't do it right—now I can do it much better than that. Give me leave, and I'll shew you the true Mechlin method (turns the cushion round, kneels down, and begins working) First you see, so—then, so—

div.stage: Enter Sir Abel, and Miss Blandford, R.3.E.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I vow, Miss Blandford, fair as I ever thought you, the air of your native land has given additional lustre to your charms!—(aside) If my wife looked so—Ah! but where can Bob be?—You must know, miss, my son is a very clever fellow! you won't find him wasting his time in boyish frivolity!—no; you will find him—(sees him)

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: (R.)Is that your son, sir?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (R.C., abashed) Yes, that's Bob!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Pray, sir, is he making lace, or is he making love?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Curse me if I can tell. (hits him with his stick) Get up, you dog! don't you see Miss Blandford?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (L.C., starting up) Zounds! how unlucky! Ma'am, your most obedient servant. (endeavours to hide the work) Curse the cushion! (throws it off)

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (L.)Oh! he has spoiled my lace!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Hush! I'll make you a thousand yards another time. (Dame retires to the cottage)You see, ma'am, I was explaining to this good woman—what—what need not be explained again—Admirably handsome, by Heaven! (aside)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Is not she, Bob?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (crossing C. to Miss B.) In your journey from the coast, I conclude you took London in your way?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh no, sir, I could not so soon venture into the beau monde; a stranger just arrived from Germany—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (C.)The very reason; the most fashionable introduction possible! But it requires no valet de place to conduct you through the purlieus of fashion, for now the way of the world is, for every one to pursue their own way; and following the fashion is — differing as much as possible from the rest of your acquaintance.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: But, surely sir, there is some distinguishing feature, by which the votaries of fashion are known?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Yes; but that varies extremely—sometimes fashionable celebrity depends on a high waist, sometimes on a low carriage, sometimes on high play, and sometimes on low breeding; last winter it rested solely on green peas!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Green peas!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Green peas! That lady was the most enchanting, who could bring the greatest quantity of green peas to her table at Christmas! the struggle was tremendous! Mrs. Rowley Powley had the best of it by five pecks and a half, but it having been unfortunately proved, that at her ball there was room to dance and eat conveniently—that no lady received a black eye, and no coachman was killed, the thing was voted decent and comfortable, and scouted accordingly.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Is comfort then incompatible with fashion?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Certainly!—Comfort in high life would be as preposterous as a lawyer's bag crammed with truth. No—it is not comfort and selection that is sought, but numbers and confusion! So that a fashionable party resembles Smithfield market,—only a good one when plentifully stocked; and ladies are reckoned by the score, like sheep, and their husbands by droves like horned cattle!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Ha, ha! — and the conversation—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (C.)Oh! like the assembly—confused, vapid, and abundant; as "How d'ye do, ma'am!—no accident at the door?—he, he!"—"Only my carriage broke to pieces."—"I hope you had not your pocket picked?"—"Won't you sit down to faro?"—"Have you many to-night?"—"A few, about six hundred!"—"Were you at Lady Overall's?"—"Oh yes; a delicious crowd, and plenty of peas, he, he!"—and thus runs the fashionable race.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p:
Yes; and a precious run it is—full gallop all the way: first they run on—then their fortune is run
through—then bills are run up—then they are run hard—then they've a run of luck—then they run out — and then they run away! But I'll forgive fashion all its follies in consideration of one of its blessed laws.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: What may that be?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: That husband and wife must never be seen together.

div.stage: Enter Thomas, R.3 E.

sp.speaker: Thomas.
sp.p: Miss Blandford, your father expects you.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: I hope I shall find him more composed.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Is Sir Philip ill?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: His spirits are extremely depressed, and since we arrived here this morning his dejection has dreadfully increased.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: But I hope we shall be able to laugh away despondency.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Sir, if you are pleased to consider my esteem as an object worth your possession, I know no way of obtaining it so certain as by your shewing every attention to my dear father. (as they are going)

div.stage: Enter Ashfield.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Dame! Dame! she be come!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (enters from cottage) Who? Susan! our dear Susan?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees—zo—come along—Oh, Sir Abel! Lady Nelly, your spouse, do order you to go to her directly!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Order! you mistake—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No, he don't—she generally prefers that word.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Adieu! Sir Abel.
sp.stage: Exeunt_ Miss Blandford and Bob Handy, R. 3 E

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (R.)Oh! if my wife had such a pretty way with her mouth!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (L.)And how does Susan look?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (C.)That's what I do want to know, zoa come along—Woo ye though—Missus, let's behave pratty—Zur if you pleaze, Dame and I will let you walk along wi' us.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: How condescending! Oh, you are a pratty behaved fellow!

div.head: Scene Second .- The interior of Farmer Ashfield's house, door of entrance, L. 2 E., a box on a table at back.
div.stage: Enter Lady Handy and Susan, L.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: (L.)My dear home, thrice welcome!—What gratitude I feel to your ladyship for this indulgence!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (R.) That's right, child!

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: And I am sure you partake my pleasure in again visiting a place, where you received every protection and kindness my parents could shew you, for, I remember, while you lived with my father—

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Child! don't put your memory to any fatigue on my account—you may transfer the remembrance of who I was, to aid your more perfect recollection of who I am.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Lady Handy?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: That's right, child!—I am not angry.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: (looking out) Ah! I see my dear father and mother coming through the garden.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Oh! now I shall be caressed to death; but I must endure the shock of their attentions.

div.stage: Enter Farmer and Dame, with Sir Abel, L.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (C.)My dear Susan!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (L. C.)My sweet child! give me a kiss.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Hald thee! Feyther first though—Well, I be as mortal glad to zee thee as never war; and how be'st thee? and how do thee like Lunnun town? it be a deadly lively place I be tould.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Is not she a sweet girl?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: That she is.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (R.C. with affected dignity) Does it occur to any one present, that Lady Handy is in the room?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Oh, Lud! I'm sure, my dear wife, I never forget that you are in the room.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Drabbitit! I overlooked Lady Nelly, sure enow; but consider, there be zome difference between thee and our own Susan! I be deadly glad to zee thee, however.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: So am I, Lady Handy.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Don't ye take it unkind I han't a buss'd thee yet; meant no slight indeed. (kisses her)

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Oh! shocking! (aside)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: No harm I do hope, zur.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: None at all.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: But dash it, Lady Nelly, what do make thee paint thy vace all over we rud ochre zoo? Be it vor thy spouse to knaw thee?—that be the way I do knaw my sheep.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: The flocks of fashion are all marked so, Farmer.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Likely! Drabbit it! thee do make a tightish kind of a ladyship zure enow.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: That you do, my lady! you remember the old house?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Aye; and all about it, doant ye? Nelly! my lady!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Oh! I'm quite shocked. Susan, child! prepare a room where I may dress before I proceed to the castle. Exit_ Susan, R. 2 E. - Ashfield and Dame retire up a little)

div.stage: Enter Bob Handy, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I don't see Susan. (crosses to Lady Handy) —I say, Dad, is that my mamma?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (L.)Yes—speak to her.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (chucking her under the chin) A fine girl, upon my soul!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (R.)Fine girl, indeed! Is this behaviour!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Oh! beg pardon, most honoured parent. (she curtsies)—-that's a cursed bad curtsey. I can teach you to make a much better curtsey than that!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: You teach me, that am old enough to—hem!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Oh! that toss of the head was very bad indeed. Look at me! That's the thing!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Am I to be insulted? Sir Abel, you know I seldom condescend to talk.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Don't say so, my lady, you wrong yourself.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: But, when I do begin, you know not where it will end.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Indeed I do not. (aside)

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: I insist on receiving all possible respect from your son.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: And you shall have it, my dear girl!—Madam, I mean.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: I vow, I am agitated to that degree—Sir Abel! my fan.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes, my dear—Bob, look here, a little contrivance of my own. While others carry swords and such like dreadful weapons in their canes, I more gallantly carry a fan. (removes the head of his cane, and draws out a fan) — A pretty thought, isn't it? (presents it to his lady)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Some difference between thic stick and mine, beant there, zur? (to Bob Handy)

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (moving away) Yes, there is.(to Lady H.) Do you call that fanning yourself? (taking the fan) My dear mama, this is the way to manœuvre a fan.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Sir, you shall find (to Bob Handy) I have power enough to make you repent this behaviour, severely repent it—Susan! Exit followed by_ Dame.)

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Bravo! passion becomes her. She does that vastly well.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes; practice makes perfect.

div.stage: Enter Susan, R. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: (R.)Did your ladyship call?—Heavens! Mr. Handy!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (R.C.)Hush! my angel! be composed! -- that letter will explain. (giving a letter, noticed by Ashfield) Lady Handy wishes to see you.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Oh, Robert!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: At present, my love, no more.
sp.stage: Exit_ Susan, followed by Ashfield, R. 1 E.)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (L.)What were you saying, sir, to that young woman?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (R.)Nothing particular, sir. Where is Lady Handy going?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: To dress.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I suppose she has found out the use of money.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p:
Yes; I'll do her the justice to say she encourages trade.—Why, do you know, Bob, my best coal pit won't find her in white muslin; —round her neck hangs a hundred acres at least; my noblest oaks have made wigs for her; my fat oxen have dwindled into Dutch pugs, and
white mice; my India bonds are transmuted into shawls and otto of roses; and a magnificent mansion has shrunk into a diamond snuff-box.

div.stage: Enter Ralph, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Ralph.
sp.p: Gentlemen, the folks be all got together, and the ploughs be ready, and——

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: We are coming.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Ploughs!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes, Bob, we are going to have a grand agricultural meeting.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Indeed!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: If I could but find a man able to manage my new-invented curricle plough, none of them would have a chance.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: My dear sir, if there be any thing on earth I can do, it is that.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: What?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I rather fancy I can plough better than any man in England.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: You don't say so! What a clever fellow he is! I say, Bob, if you would—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No! I can't condescend.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Condescend! why not?—much more creditable, let me tell you, than gallopping a maggot for a thousand, or eating a live cat, or any other fashionable achievement.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: So it is—Egad! I will—I will carry off the prize of industry.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: But should you lose, Bob!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I lose! that's vastly well!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: True, with my curricle plough you could hardly fail.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: With my superior skill, Dad—Then, I say, how the newspapers will teem with the account.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: That universal genius, Handy, junior, with a plough——

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Stop—invented by that ingenious machinist, Handy, senior.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Gained the prize against the first husbandmen in Hampshire—Let our Bond-street butterflies emulate the example of Handy, junior.—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: And let old city grubs cultivate the field of science, like Handy, senior—Ecod! I am so happy!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Ah! there comes a damper.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Courage! you have many resources of happiness.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Have I? I should be very glad to know them.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: In the first place you possess an excellent temper.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: So much the worse; for if I had a bad one, I should be the better able to conquer hers.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: You enjoy good health.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: So much the worse; for if I were ill, she wouldn't come near me.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Then you are rich—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: So much the worse; for had I been poor, she would not have married me. But I, say, Bob, if you gain the prize, I'll have a patent for my plough.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (without, R.) Sir Abel! I say—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Father, could not you get a patent for stopping that sort of noise?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: If I could, what a sale it would have!—No, Bob, a patent has been obtained for the only thing that will silence her—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Aye—What's that?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (in a whisper) A coffin! hush!—I'm coming, my dear.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Ha, ha, ha!

div.stage: Enter Ashfield and Dame, R. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I tell ye, I zee'd un gi' Susan a letter, an I dan't like it a bit.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Nor I: if shame should come to the poor child—I say, Tummus, what would Mrs. Grundy say then?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Dom Mrs. Grundy; what would my poor wold heart zay? but I be bound it be all innocence.

div.stage: Enter Henry, L.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Ah, Henry! we have not seen thee at home all day.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: And I do zomehow fanzie things dan't go zo clever when thee'rt away from farm.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: My mind has been greatly agitated.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, won't thee go and zee the ploughing match?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Tell me, will not those who obtain prizes be introduced to the castle?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, and feasted in the great hall.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: My good friend, I wish to become a candidate.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: You, Henry!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: It is time I exerted the faculties Heaven has bestowed on me; and though my heavy fate crushes the proud hopes this heart conceives, still let me prove myself worthy of the place Providence has assigned me. Will you furnish me with the means?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Will I!—Thou shalt ha' the best plough in the parish—I wish it were all gould for thy zake—and better cattle there can't be noowhere.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Thanks, my good friend—my benefactor—I have little time for preparation—So receive my gratitude, and farewell.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: A blessing go with thee!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I zay, Henry, take Jolly, and Smiler, and Captain, but dan't ye take thic lazy beast Genius—I'll be shot if having vive load an acre on my wheat land could please me more.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Tummus, here comes Susan reading the letter.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: How pale she do look! dan't she?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Ah! poor thing!—If——

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Hauld thy tongue, woolye?

div.stage: Enter Susan, reading the letter, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Is it possible? Can the man to whom I've given my heart write thus:—"I am compelled to marry Miss Blandford; but my love for my Susan is unalterable. I hope she will not, for an act of necessity, cease to think with tenderness on her faithful Robert."——Oh man! ungrateful man! it is from our bosoms alone you derive your power; how cruel then to use it, in fixing in those bosoms endless sorrow and despair!——"Still think with tenderness"—Base, dishonourable insinuation—He might have allowed me to esteem him. (locks up the letter in a box on the table, and exit weeping, R. 1 E.)

div.stage: (Ashfield and_ Dame come forward)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Poor thing!—What can be the matter—She lock'd up the letter in thic box, and then began to cry. (looks at the box)

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Yes, Tummus; she locked it in that box sure enough.
sp.stage: (shakes a bunch of keys that hangs at her side)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: What be doing, Dame? what be doing?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (with affected indifference) Nothing; I was only touching these keys. (they look at the box and keys significantly)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: A good tightish bunch!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Yes; they are of all sizes. (they look as before)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Indeed!—Well—Eh!—Dame, why dan't ye speak? thou canst chatter fast enow zometimes.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Nay, Tummus—I dare say—if—thee knows best—but I think I could find——

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, eh!— thee can just try, thee know (greatly agitated) Thee can try, just vor the vun on't: but mind, dan't thee make a noise. (she opens it) Why, thee hasn't opened it?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Nay, Tummus! thee told me!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Did I?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: There be the letter!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, why do ye gi't to I?—I dan't want it, I'm sure. (taking it—he turns it over—she eyes it eagerly—he is about to open it)—She's coming! she's coming! (he conceals the letter, they tremble violently) No, she's gone into t'other room. (they hang their heads dejectedly, then look at each other) What mun that feyther an mother be doing, that do blush and tremble at their own dater's coming. Dang it, has she desarv'd it of us? Did she ever deceive us? Were she not always the most open-hearted, dutifullest, kindest—and thee to goa like a dom'd spy, and open her box, poor thing!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Nay, Tummus——

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: You did—I zaw you do it myself!— thee look like a thief, now, thee do. Hush!—no—Dame—here be the letter—I won't read a word on't; put it where thee vound it, and as thee vound it.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: With all my heart.
sp.stage: (she returns the letter to the box)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p:
(embraces her) Now I can wi' pleasure hug my wold wife, and look my child in the vace again—I'll call
her, and ax her about it; and if she dan't speak without disguisement, I'll be bound to be shot. Dame, be the colour of sheame off my face yet? I never zeed thee look ugly before. (calls, R.) Susan, my dear Sue, come here a bit, woollye?

div.stage: Enter Susan.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Yes, my dear father.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Sue, we do wish to give thee a bit of admonishing and parent-like conzultation.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: I hope I have ever attended to your admonitions.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, bless thee, I do believe thee hast, lamb; but we all want our memories jogg'd a bit, or why else do parson preach us all to sleep every Zunday—Zo thic be the topic—Dame and I, Sue, did zee a letter gi'd to thee, and thee—bursted into tears, and lock'd un up in thic box—and then Dame and I—we—that's all.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: My dear father, if I concealed the contents of that letter from your knowledge, it was because I did not wish your heart to share in the pain mine feels.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (to his wife) Dang it, didn't I tell thee zoo?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Nay, Tummus, did I say otherwise?

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Believe me, my dear parents, my heart never gave birth to a thought my tongue feared to utter.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: There, the very words I zaid?

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: If you wish to see the letter, I will shew it to you.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Here's a key will open it.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (aside) Drabbit it, hold thy tongue, thou wold fool? (aside)No, Susan. I'll not zee it—I'll believe my child.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: You shall not find your confidence ill-placed—it is true the gentleman declared he loved me; it is equally true that declaration was not unpleasing to me—Alas! it is also true, that his letter contains sentiments disgraceful to himself, and insulting to me.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Drabbit it, if I'd knaw'd that, when we were cudgelling a bit, I wou'd ha' lapt my stick about his ribs pratty tightish, I wou'd.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Pray, father, don't you resent his conduct to me.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: What! mayn't I lather un a bit?

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Oh, no! I've the strongest reasons to the contrary!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, Sue, I won't—I'll behave as pratty as I always do—but it be time to go to the green, and zee the fine zights. (Dame shakes her keys) How I do hate the noise of this dom'd bunch of keys. But bless thee, my child, dan't forget that vartue to a young woman be for all the world like—like—dang it, I ha' gotten it all in my head; but zomehow—I can't talk it—but vartue be to a young woman what corn be to a blade o'wheat, do you zee; (takes an ear of corn from his hat) for while the corn be there it be glorious to the eye, and it be called the staff of life; but take that treasure away, and what do remain? why nought but the worthless straw that man and beast do tread upon.
sp.stage: Exeunt Susan, R. Dame and Farmer, L.

div.head: Scene Third
div.stage: An extensive view of a cultivated country—A ploughed field in the centre, in which are seen six different ploughs and horses— at one side a handsome tent; a number of country people assembled.
Enter Ashfield and Dame.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Make way! make way for the gentry! and, do ye hear, behave pratty as I do—Dang thee, stond back, or I'll knack thee down, I wool.

div.stage: Enter Sir Abel, and Miss Blandford, with Thomas and William, R.U.E.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: It is very kind of you to honour our rustic festivities with your presence.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Pray, Sir Abel, where's your son?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: What! Bob? Oh, you'll see him presently—(nodding significantly)—Here are the prize medals; and if you will condescend to present them, I'm sure they'll be worn with additional pleasure.—I say, you'll see Bob presently.—Well, Farmer, is it all over?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p:
Ees, zur; the acres be plough'd and the ground judg'd; and the young lads be coming down to receive their reward. Heartily welcome, miss, to your native land; hope you be as pleased to zee we as we be to zee
thee. Mortal beautizome to be zure (a shout without) They be coming. Now, Henry!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Now you'll see Bob!—now my dear boy, Bob, here he comes. (shouts)

div.stage: Enter Henry, L.U.E.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: 'Tis he, he has don't—Dang you all, why dan't ye shout? (huzza)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Why, zounds, where's Bob?—I don't see Bob—Bless me, what has become of Bob and my plough? (retires and takes out his glass)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (L.C.)Well, Henry, there be the prize, and there be the fine lady that will gi' it thee.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: (L.)Tell me who is that lovely creature?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: The dater of Sir Philip Blandford.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: What exquisite sweetness! Ah! should the father but resemble her, I shall have but little to fear from his severity.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Miss, thic be the young man that ha got'n the goulden prize.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: This; I always thought ploughmen were coarse, vulgar creatures, but he seems handsome and diffident.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, quite pratty behaved—it were I that teached un.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: What's your name?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Henry!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: And your family!

div.stage: (Henry, in grief, turns away, and leans on the shoulder of Ashfield.)

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (apart to Miss B.) Madam, I beg pardon, but nobody knows about his parentage; and when it is mentioned, poor boy! he takes on sadly. He has lived at our house ever since we had the farm, and we have had an allowance for him—small enough to be sure—but, good lad! he was always welcome to share what we had.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: (to Henry) Pray pardon me, sir, I would not insult an enemy, much less one I am inclined to admire—(giving her hand, then withdraws it)—to esteem. You shall go to the Castle—my father shall protect you.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p:
Generous lady! to merit his esteem is the
fondest wish of my heart—to be your slave, the proudest aim of my ambition.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Receive your merited reward. (he kneels—Thomas and William advance, and present the medals to Miss Blandford — she places the medal round his neck—then goes up R.)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (advances) I can't see Bob; pray, sir, do you happen to know what is become of my Bob?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Sir!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Did not you see a remarkable clever plough, and a young man——

sp.speaker: Henry.
Bob.
sp.p: (without) How dare you laugh?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: That's Bob's voice! (laughing again)

div.stage: Enter Bob Handy in a smock frock, cocked hat, and a handle of a plough in his hand, L.U.E

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Dare to laugh again, and I'll knock you down with this. Ugh! how infernally hot! (walks about)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Why, Bob, where have you been?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I don't know where I have been.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: And what have you got in your hand?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: What! All I could keep of your nonsensical ricketty plough. (walks about, Sir Abel following)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (R.)Come, none of that, sir. Don't abuse my plough, to cover your ignorance, sir? where is it, sir? and where are my famous Leicestershire horses, sir?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Where? ha, ha, ha! I'll tell you as nearly as I can — ha, ha! What's the name of the next county?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (L.)It be called Wiltshire, zur.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Then, dad, upon the nicest calculation I am able to make, they are at this moment engaged in the very patriotic act of ploughing Salisbury plain; ha ha! I saw them fairly over that hill, full gallop, with the curricle plough at their heels. But never mind, father, you must again set your invention to work, and I my toilet:—rather a deranged figure to appear before a lady in. (fiddles heard) Hey day! What! are you going to dance?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur; I suppose you can sheake a leg a bit?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I fancy I can dance every possible step, from the pas ruse to the war-dance of the Catabaws.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Likely.—I do hope, miss, you'll join your honest neighbours; they'll be deadly hurt an' you won't jig it a bit wi' un.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: (R.)With all my heart.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Bob's an excellent dancer.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: I dare say he is, sir; but on this occasion, I think I ought to dance with the young man, who gained the prize—I think it would be the most pleasant—most proper, I mean; and I am glad you agree with me.(crosses to Henry, L.) So, sir, if you'll accept my hand.(they go up, L.)

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Very pleasantly settled, upon my soul!—Bob, won't you dance?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I dance!—no, I'll look at them—I'll quietly look on.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Egad now, as my wife's away, I'll try to find a pretty girl, and make one among them.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: That's hearty!—Come, Dame, hang the rheumatics!—Now, lads and lasses, behave pratty, and strike up.
sp.stage: A dance — two or four couples lead down to front — whole turn — hands across — cast off — whole and half turn — lady's chain — hands round — Bov Handy looks on a little, then begins to move his legs — then sticks the plough handle (which has a spike on it) into ground, L., places his cocked hat on it, and dances round it — at hands round — seizes the fiddle, runs into the circle and plays 'till

div.head: SCENE FIRST.
div.stage: An Esplanade before the Castle.
Emter Sir Philip, R., meeting Evergreen, L.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Are we secure from observation?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Then tell me, does the boy live?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: He does.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Has care been taken he may not blast me with his presence?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Hush! your daughter.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Leave me—we'll speak anon.

div.stage: Enter Miss Blandford, L.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Dear father, I came the moment I heard you wished to see me.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: My good child, I fear my wish for thy company deprives thee of much pleasure.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: (L.)Oh no! what pleasure can be equal to that of giving you happiness? Am I not rewarded in seeing your eyes beam with pleasure on me?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: 'Tis the pale reflection of the lustre I see sparkle there. But, tell me, did your lover gain the prize?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes, papa.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Few men of his rank—

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh! you mean Mr. Handy?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: No; he did not.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Then, whom did you mean?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Did you say lover? I—I mistook.—No; a young man called Henry obtained the prize?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: And how did Mr. Handy succeed?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh! It was so ridiculous! I will tell you, papa, what happened to him.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: To Mr. Handy?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes; as soon as the contest was over Henry presented himself. I was surprised at seeing a young man so handsome and elegant as Henry is.—Then I placed the medal round Henry's neck, and was told, that poor Henry—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Henry!—So, my love, this is your account of Mr. Robert Handy!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes, papa—no, papa—he came afterwards, dressed so ridiculously, that even Henry could not help smiling.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Henry again!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Then we had a dance.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Of course you danced with your lover?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes, papa.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: How does Mr. Handy dance?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh! he did not dance till—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: You danced with your lover?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes—no papa!—Somebody said (I don't know who) that I ought to dance with Henry, because—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Still Henry! Oh! some rustic boy. My dear child, you talk as if you loved this Henry.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh! no, papa—and I am certain he don't love me.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Indeed!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes, papa; for, when he touched my hand, he trembled as if I terrified him; and instead of looking at me as you do, who I am sure love me, when our eyes met, he withdrew his and cast them on the ground.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: And these are the reasons, which make you conclude he does not love you?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes, papa.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: And probably you could adduce proof equally convincing that you don't love him?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh, yes—quite; for in the dance he sometimes paid attention to other young women, and I was so angry with him! Now, you know, papa, I love you—and I am sure I should not have been angry with you had you done so.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: But one question more—Do you think Mr. Handy loves you?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: I have never thought about it, papa.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I am satisfied!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Yes, I knew I should convince you.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Oh, Love! malign and subtle tyrant, how falsely art thou painted blind! 'Tis thy votaries are so; for what but blindness can prevent their seeing thy poisoned shaft, which is for ever doomed to rankle in the victim's heart.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p:
Oh! now I am certain I am not in love; for I feel no rankling at my heart. I feel the softest, sweetest sensation I ever experienced. But, papa, you must come to the lawn. I don't know why, but to-day
nature seems enchanting; the birds sing more sweetly, and the flowers give more perfume.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Such was the day my youthful fancy pictured! How did it close!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: I promised Henry your protection.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Indeed! that was much. Well I will see your rustic here. This infant passion must be crushed. Poor girl! some artless boy has caught thy youthful fancy!

div.stage: Enter Henry and Ashfield, L.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well! here thee'rt going to make thy bow to Sir Philip. I zay, if he should take a fancy to thee, thou'lt come to farm, and zee us zometimes, wo'tn't, Henry?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Tell me, is that Sir Philip Blandford, who leans on that lady's arm?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (R.)I don't know, by reason d'ye zee, I never zeed'un. Well, good bye! I declare thee doz look quite grand with thic golden prize about thy neck, vor all the world like the lords with their stars, that do come to theas pearts to pickle their skins in the zalt zea ocean! Good bye, Henry!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: He approaches! why this agitation? I wish, yet dread, to meet him.

div.stage: Enter Sir Philip, Miss Blandford, and two Servants, R.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: The joy your tenantry display at seeing you again must be truly grateful to you.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: No, my child; for I feel I do not merit it. — Alas! I can see no orphans clothed with my beneficence, no anguish assuaged by my care.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Then I am sure my dear father wishes to show his kind intentions. So I will begin by placing one under his protection (she leads down Henry — Sir Philip, on seeing him, starts, then becomes greatly agitated.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (R.)Ah! do my eyes deceive me! — No; it must be him! Such was the face his father wore!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p:
(L.)Spake you of my father? Oh sir! tell me! on my knees I ask it! — Do my parents live? Bless me with my father's name, and my days shall pass in
active gratitude—my nights in prayers for you. Sir Philip views him with severe contempt. Do not mock my misery! Have you a heart?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Yes; of marble. Cold and obdurate to the world—ponderous and painful to myself—Quit my sight for ever!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Go, Henry, and save me from my father's curse.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: I obey: cruel as the command is, I obey it—I shall often look at this, (touching the medal) and think on the blissful moment when your hand placed it there.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! tear it from his breast.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Sooner take my life! It is the first honour I have earned, and it is no mean one; for it assigns me the first rank among the sons of industry! This is my claim to the sweet rewards of honest labour! This will give me competence, — nay more — enable me to despise your tyranny!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Rash boy, mark! — Avoid me, and be secure.—Repeat this intrusion, and my vengeance shall pursue thee —

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: I defy its power! You are in England, sir, where the man, who bears about him an upright heart, bears a charm too potent for tyranny to humble. Can your frown wither up my youthful vigour? — No! Can your malediction disturb the slumbers of a quiet conscience? — No! Can your breath stifle in my heart the adoration it feels for that pitying angel? — Oh, no!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Wretch! you shall be taught the difference between us!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: I feel it now!— proudly feel it! You hate the man that never wronged you—I could love the man that injures me. You meanly triumph o'er a worm—I make a giant tremble.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Take him from my sight! Why am I not obeyed?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Henry, if you wish my hate should not accompany my father's, instantly begone.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Oh, pity me!

div.stage: Miss Blandford looks after him—Sir Philip, exhausted, leans on his servants.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Supported by my servants! I thought I had a daughter!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: O, you have, my father! one that loves you better than life!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Leave us.

Emma, if you feel, as I fear you do, love for that youth—mark my words! When the dove woos for its mate the ravenous kite; — when Nature's fixed antipathies mingle in sweet concord, — then, and not till then, hope to be united.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: O Heaven!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Have you not promised me the disposal of your hand?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Alas! my father! I didn't then know the difficulty of obedience!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Hear, then, the reasons why I demand compliance. You think I hold these rich estates—Alas, the shadow only, not the substance.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Explain, my father!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Tis now twenty years, since I sueceeded as elder sdn to the ample fortune of my father.’ I had a brother, whom as my life I loved, I felt no joys, but what he shared, and the moment the law gave me power, I divided, equal portions, my father’s large possessions, one of which I with joy presented to my brother.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: ’Twas like my noble ‘father

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p:
You shall hear how I was rewarded. I loved, deeply, passionately, and. fondly thought I was beloved again. The-evening previous to my intended marriage, with a mind serene as the departing sun whose morning ray was to light me to my happiness, I sought a favourite tree, where, lover-like I had carved the name of my destined bride; and with every nerve braced to the tone of ecstacy, I was proceeding to wound the bark with a still deeper impression of her name—when there, where heaven heard and registered our vows, what did I behold ? the only two beings who had wound themselves about my heart, my brother and my betrothed bride, were sitting clasped in each other's arms. I heard arrangements made for instant flight—that morning they had been secretly married; the perjured brother pitied me, and told her he had prepared. a vessel to bear her for ever from my sight.
Madness seized me, the knife was in my grasp, I sprung upon them, with one hand tore the faithless woman from his vile embrace, and with the other I stabbed my brother to the heart.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Oh, my father!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Whom think you was the offspring of these treacherous friends?

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Henry.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Now ask me, have I cause to hate him? After the deed was done, I hurried from my native land, with a heart lacerated by every wound, that the falsehood of others, or my own conscience could inflict, and madly plunged into every dissipation to stupify my maddened brain; I rushed to the gaming table, and soon became the dupe of villains.—My ample fortune was lost. The man, who, by his superior genius, stood possessed of all the mortgages and securities of which I had been plundered was one Morrington. Collecting the scattered remnant of my fortune, I wandered, wretched and desolate, till, in a peaceful village, I first beheld thy mother — humble in birth, but exalted in virtue. The morning after our marriage she received a packet, containing these words: "The reward of virtuous love, presented by a repentant villain;" and which also contained bills and notes to the high amount of ten thousand pounds.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: And no name?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: None; nor could I ever guess at the generous donor. Thus circumstanced, this good man, Sir Abel Handy, proposed to unite our families by marriage. Yet still another wonder remains.—When I arrive, I find no claim whatever has been made, either by Morrington or his agents. What am I to think? Can Morrington have perished, and with him his large claims to my property? Or, does he withhold the blow, to make it fall more heavily? nit let it pass — my bitter task is ended — yours begins; you must forget, nay, hate this boy.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Bitter indeed; oh my dear father, if he be the worthy child of such ungrateful parents, does he not deserve my pity rather than my hate?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I have said it, so speak no more; my word and yours is plighted to Sir Abel Handy; prepare to wed his son.

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: Father, be composed; let me wipe away those drops of anguish from your brow:—I will be all you wish—indeed I will, my father!

div.stage: Enter Evergreen, R.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Was ever any thing so unlucky! Henry to come to the Castle and meet Sir Philip! He should have consulted me; I shall be blamed—but, thank Heaven, I am innocent.

div.stage: Sir Abel and Lady Handy without, R.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: I will be treated with respect.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: You shall, my dear.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: But how! but how, Sir Abel? I repeat it—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: For the fiftieth time.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Your son conducts himself with an insolence I won't endure; but you are ruled by him, you have no will of your own.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I have not, indeed.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: How contemptible!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Why, my dear, this is the case—I am like the ass in the fable; and if I am doomed to carry a packsaddle, it is not much matter who drives me.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: To yield your power to those the law allows you to govern!—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Is very weak, indeed.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Lady Handy, your very humble servant; I heartily congratulate you, madam, on your marriage with this worthy gentleman—Sir, I give you joy.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Not before 'tis wanted.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Aye, my lady, this match makes up for the imprudence of your first.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Hem!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Eh! What!—what's that—Eh! what do you mean?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: I mean, sir—that Lady Handy's former husband—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Former husband!—Why, my dear, I never knew—Eh!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: A mumbling old blockhead!—Didn't you, Sir Abel? Yes; I was rather married many years ago; but my husband went abroad and died.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Died, did he?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Yes, sir, he was a servant in the Castle.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Indeed! So he died—poor fellow!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: What, you are sure he died, are you?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Don't you hear?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Poor fellow! neglected perhaps—had I known it, he should have had the best advice money could have got.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: You seem sorry.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Why, you would not have me pleased at the death of your husband, would you?—a good kind of man?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Yes; a faithful fellow—rather ruled his wife too severely.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Did he! (apart to Evergreen) Pray do you happen to recollect his manner!—Could you just give a hint of the way he had?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Do you want to tyrannize over my poor tender heart?—'Tis too much! (sits in a chair)

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Bless me! Lady Handy is ill—Salts! salts!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Here are salts, or aromatic vinegar, or essence of—

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Any—any.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Bless me, I can't find the key!

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Pick the lock.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: It can't be picked, it is a patent lock.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Then break it open, sir.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: It can't be broke open—it is a contrivance of my own—you see, here comes a horizontal bolt, which acts upon a spring, therefore—

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (starts up) I may die, while you are describing a horizontal bolt. Do you think you shall close your eyes for a week for this?

div.stage: Enter Sir Philip Blandford, L.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: What has occasioned this disturbance?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Ask that gentleman.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Sir, I am accused—

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Convicted! convicted!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Well, I will not argue with you about words—because I must bow to your superior practice—But, sir—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Pshaw! Apart. Lady Handy, some of your people were inquiring for you.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (crossing, L.)Thank you, sir. Come, Sir Abel.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes, my lady—I say (to Evergreen) couldn't you give me a hint of the way he had?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Sir Abel!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Coming, my soul!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: So! you have well obeyed my orders in keeping this Henry from my presence.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: I was not to blame, master.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (L.)Has Farmer Ashfield left the Castle?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: No, sir.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Send him hither.
That boy must be driven far, far from my sight—but where?—no matter! the world is large enough.
—Come hither. I believe you hold a farm of mine.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (L.)Ees, zur, I do, at your zarvice.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (R.)I hope a profitable one?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Zometimes it be, zur. But thic year it be all t'other way as 'twur; but I do hope, as our landlords have a tightish big lump of the good, they'll be zo kind hearted as to take a little bit of the bad.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: It is but reasonable—I conclude then you are in my debt.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur, I be—at your zarvice.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: How much?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I do owe ye a hundred and fifty pounds—at your zarvice.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Which you can't pay?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Not a varthing, zur—at your zarvice.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Well, I am willing to give you every indulgence.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Be you, zur? that be deadly kind. Dear heart! it will make my auld dame quite young again, and I don't think helping a poor man will do your honour's health any harm—I don't indeed, zur—I had a thought of speaking to your worship about it—but then, thinks I, the gentleman, mayhap, be one of those that do like to do a good turn, and not have a word zaid about it—zo, zur, if you had not mentioned what I owed you, I am zure I never should—should not, indeed, zur.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Nay, I will wholly acquit you of the debt, on condition—

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: On condition, I say, you instantly turn out that boy—that Henry.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Turn out Henry!—Ha, ha, ha! Excuse my tittering, zur; but you bees making your vun of I, zure.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I am not apt to trifle—send him instantly from you, or take the consequences.(crosses to R.)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Turn out Henry! I do vow I shou'dn't knaw how to zet about it—I should not, indeed, zur.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: You hear my determination. If you disobey, you know what will follow—I'll leave you to reflect on it.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, zur, I'll argufy the topic, and then you may wait upon me, and I'll tell ye. (makes the motion of turning out)—I should be deadly awkward at it, vor zartain—however, I'll put the case—Well! I goes whiztling whoam—noa, drabbit it! I shou'dn't be able to whiztle a bit, I'm zure. Well! I goas whoam, and I zees Henry zitting by my wife, mixing up someit to comfort the wold zoul, and take away the pain of her rheumatics—Very well! Then Henry places a chair vor I by the vire zide, and says—-"Varmer, the horses be fed, the sheep be folded, and you have nothing to do but to zit down, smoke your pipe, and be happy!" Very well! (becomes affected) Then I zays—"Henry, you be poor and friendless, zo you must turn out of my houze directly." Very well! then my wife stares at I—reaches her hand towards the vire place, and throws the poker at my head. Very well! then Henry gives a kind of aguish shake, and getting up, sighs from the bottom of his heart—then holding up his head like a king, zays—"Varmer, I have too long been a burden to you—Heaven protect you, as you have me—Farewell! I go." Then I says, "If thee doez I'll be domn'd!" (with great energy) Hollo! you Mister Sir Philip! you may come in.—

div.stage: Enter Sir Philip Blandford, R.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Zur, I have argufied the topic, and it wou'dn't be pratty—zo I can't.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Can't! absurd!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Well, zur, there is but another word—I won't.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Indeed!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: No, zur, I won't—I'd zee myself hang'd first, and you too, zur—I would indeed.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: You refuse then to obey?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I do, zur—at your zarvice.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Then the law must take its course.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I be zorry for that too—I be, indeed, zur, but if corn wou'dn't grow I cou'dn't help it; it wer'n't poison'd by the hand that zow'd it. Thic hand, zur, be as free from guilt as your own.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Oh!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: It were never held out to clinch a hard bargain, nor will it turn a good lad out into the wide wicked world, because he be poorish a bit. I be zorry you be offended, zur, quite; but come what would, I'll never hit thic hand against here, but when I be zure that zumeit at inside will jump against it with pleasure. (bowing) I do hope you'll repent of all your zins—I do, indeed, zur; and if you shou'd, I'll come and zee you again as friendly as ever. I wool, indeed, zur.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Your repentance will come too late!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Thank ye, zur—Good morning to you—I do hope I have made myzel agreeable—and so I'll go whoam.

div.head: SCENE FIRST.—
div.stage: A room in Ashfield's House.
Dame Ashfield at work with her needle— and Henry places a chair C. and sits.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Come, come, Henry, you'll fret yourself ill, child. If Sir Philip will not be kind to you, you are but where you were.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: My peace of mind is gone for ever. Sir Philip may have cause for hate;—spite of his unkindness to me, my heart seeks to find excuses for him— for, oh! that heart doats on his lovely daughter. (goes up)

div.stage: Enter Ashfield, L. musing; he stumbles against a chair.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Tummus, my dear Tummus, what's the matter?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: It be lucky vor he I be's zoo pratty behaved, or dom if I—

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (R.)Who—what?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Nothing at all; where's Henry?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: (advancing, L.)Here, farmer.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (C.)Thee wouldn't leave us, Henry, wou't?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Leave you! What, leave you now, when by my exertion I can pay off part of the debt of gratitude I owe you? oh, no!

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Nay, it were not vor that I axed, I promise thee; come, gi'us thy hand on't then. (shaking hands) Now, I'll tell ye. Zur Philip did send vor I about the money I do owe 'un, and said as how he'd make all straight between us——

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: That was kind.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Yes, deadly kind. Make all strait on condition I did turn Henry out o'my doors.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: What!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Where will his hatred cease?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: And what did you say, Tummus?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Why I zivelly tould un, if it were agreeable to he to behave like a brute, it were agreeable to I to behave like a man.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: That was right. I wou'd have told him a great deal more.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ah! likely. Then a' zaid I shou'd ha' a bit a laa vor my pains.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: And do you imagine I will see you suffer on my account? No—I will remove this hated form—— (going, L.)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: No, but thee shat'un—thee shat'un—I tell thee. Thee have givun me thy hand on't, and dom'me if thee sha't budge one step out of this house. Drabbit it! what can he do? he can't send us to jail. Why, I have corn will zell for half the money I do owe'un—and han't I cattle and sheep? deadly lean to be zure—and han't I a thumping zilver watch, almost as big as thy head? and Dame here a got——How many silk gowns have thee got, dame!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Three, Tummus—and sell them all—and I'll go to church in a stuff one—and let Mrs. Grundy turn up her nose as much as she pleases.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Oh, my friends, my heart is full. Yet a day will come, when this heart will prove its gratitude.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: That day, Henry, is every day.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Dang it! never be down hearted. I do know as well as can be, zome good luck will turn up. All the way I comed whoam I looked to vind a purse in the path. But I didn't though. (a knocking at the door, L.)

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Ah! here they are, coming to sell, I suppose.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Lettun—lettun zeize and zell; we ha gotten here (striking his breast) what we won't zell, and they can't zell. (knocking again) Come in—dang it, don't ye be shy.

div.stage: Enter Morrington and Gerald, L.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Ah! the strangers I saw this morning. These are not officers of law.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Noa!—Walk in, gemmen. Glad to zee ye, wi' all my heart and zoul. Come, dame, spread a cloth, bring out cold meat, and a mug of beer.

div.stage: (Dame goes off, R., and returns with table-cloth, which she prepares to spread)

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: That is the boy. (Morrington nods)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Take a chair, zur.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: I thank, and admire your hospitality. Don't trouble yourself, good woman. I am not inclined to eat.
