sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: That be the case here. To-day none o'we be auver hungry: misfortin be apt to stay the stomach confoundedly.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Has misfortune reached this humble dwelling?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Ees, zur. I do think vor my part it do work its way in every where.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Well, never despair.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I never do, zur. It is not my way. When the sun do shine I never think of voul weather, not I; and when it do begin to rain, I always think that's a zure zign it will give auver.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Is that young man your son?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: No, zur—I do wish he were wi' all my heart and zoul.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Sir, remember.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Doubt not my prudence. Young man, your appearance interests me; how can I serve you?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: By informing me who are my parents.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: That I cannot do.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Then, by removing from me the hatred of Sir Philip Blandford.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Does Sir Philip hate you?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: With such severity, that even now he is about to ruin these worthy creatures, because they have protected me.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Indeed! misfortune has made him cruel. That should not be.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Noa, it should not, indeed, zur.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: It shall not be.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Shan't it, zur? But how shan't it?

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: I will prevent it.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Wool ye faith, and troth? Now, dame, did not I zay zome good luck would turn up?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Oh, sir, did I hear you rightly? Will you preserve my friends?—will you avert the cruel arm of power, and make the virtuous happy? my tears must thank you. (taking his hand)

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Young man, you oppress me. Forbear! I do not merit thanks—pay your gratitude where you are sure 'tis due—to Heaven. Observe me—here is a bond of Sir Philip Blandford's for £1,000, do you present it to him, and obtain a discharge for the debt of this worthy man. The rest is at your own disposal—no thanks.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: But, sir, to whom am I thus highly indebted?

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: My name is Morrington. At present that information must suffice.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Morrington.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Zur, if I may be so bold—

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Nay, friend——

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Don't be angry, I hadn't thanked you, zur, nor I won't.—Only, zur, I were going to ax, when you wou'd call again. You shall have my stamp note vor the money, you shall, indeed, zur. And in the mean time, I do hope you'll take zomeit in way of remembrance as 'twere.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Will your honour put a couple of turkeys in your pocket?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Or pop a ham under your arm? don't ye zay no, if it's agreeable.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Farewell, good friends, I shall repeat my visit soon.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: The sooner the better.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Good bye to ye, zur,—Dame and I wool go to work as merry as crickets. Good bye, Henry.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: Heaven bless your honour—and I hope you will carry as much joy away with you, as you leave behind you—I do indeed.

sp.speaker: Mor.
sp.p: Young man, proceed to the castle, and demand an audience of Sir Philip Blandford. In your way thither, I'll instruct you further. Give me your hand.

div.stage: Exeunt Morrington and Henry, L door, the others grouping at the door.

div.head: Scene Second
div.stage: Apartment in the Castle (2nd grooves).
Enter Sir Philip Blandford, R., Evergreen, L.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: (L.)My dear master, I am a petitioner to you.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (R.)None possesses a better claim to my favour—ask, and receive.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: I thank you, sir. The unhappy Henry.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: What of him?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.stage: (Sir Philip turns from him with resentment.)
sp.p: Nay, be not angry, he is without, and entreats to be admitted.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I will not again behold him.

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: I am sorry you refuse me, as it compels me to repeat his words: "If," said he, "Sir Philip denies my humble request, tell him, I demand to see him."

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Demand to see me! well, his high command shall be obeyed then (sarcastically). Bid him approach.

div.stage: Enter Henry.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: By what title, sir, do you thus intrude on me?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: By one of an imperious nature, the title of a creditor.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I your debtor!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Yes; for you owe me justice. You, perhaps, withhold from me the inestimable treasure of a parent's blessing.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: To the business that brought you hither.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Thus then—I believe this is your signature.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! (recovering himself) It is—

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Affixed to a bond of £1,000, which by assignment is mine. By virtue of this I discharge the debt of your worthy tenant Ashfield! who, it seems, was guilty of the crime of vindicating the injured, and protecting the unfortunate. Now, Sir Philip, the retribution my hate demands is, that what remains of this obligation may not be now paid to me, but wait your entire convenience and leisure.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: No! that must not be.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Oh, sir! why thus oppress an innocent man?—why spurn from you a heart, that pants to serve you? No answer, farewell. (going)

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Hold—one word before we part—tell me—I dread to ask it (aside)—How came you possessed of this bond?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: A stranger, whose kind benevolence stept in and saved—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: His name?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Morrington.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Fiend! Has he caught me!—You have seen this Morrington?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Did he speak of me?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p:
He did—and of your daughter. "Conjure him," said he, "not to sacrifice the lovely Emma by a marriage
her heart revolts at. Tell him, the life and fortune of a parent are not his own; he holds them but in trust for his offspring. Bid him reflect, that, while his daughter merits the brightest rewards a father can bestow, she is by that father doomed to the harshest fate tyranny can inflict."

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Did he say who caused this sacrifice?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: He told me you had been duped of your fortune by sharpers.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Aye, he knows that well. Young man, mark me:—This Morrington, whose precepts wear the face of virtue, and whose practice seems benevolence, was the chief of the hellish banditti that ruined me.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Is it possible?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: That bond you hold in your hand was obtained by robbery. Not by the thief who encountering you as a man, stakes life against life, but by that most cowardly villain, who, in the moment when reason sleeps, and passion is roused, draws his snares around you, and hugs you to your ruin.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: On your soul, is Morrington that man?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: On my soul, he is.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Thus, then, I annihilate the act—and thus I tread upon a villain's friendship. (tearing the bond)

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Rash boy! what have you done?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: An act of justice to Sir Philip Blandford.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: For which you claim my thanks?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Sir, I am thanked already—here. (pointing to his heart) Curse on such wealth; compared with its possession, poverty is splendour. Fear not for me—I shall not feel the piercing cold; for in that man, whose heart beats warmly for his fellow creatures, the blood circulates with freedom. My food shall be what few of the pampered sons of greatness can boast of, the luscious bread of independence; and the opiate, that brings me sleep, will be the recollection of the day passed in innocence.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Noble boy!—Oh! Blandford!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Ah!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: What have I said?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: You called me Blandford.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: 'Twas error—'twas madness.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Blandford! a thousand hopes and fears rush on my heart. Disclose to me my birth—be it what it may, I am your slave for ever. Refuse me, you create a foe, firm and implacable as——

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! am I threatened? Do not extinguish the spark of pity my breast is warmed with.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: I will not. Oh! forgive me.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Yes, on one condition—leave me.—Ah! some one approaches. Begone, I insist—I entreat.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: That word has charmed me! I obey: Sir Philip, you may hate, but you shall respect, me.

div.stage: Enter Bob Handy, R.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (R.)At last, thank Heaven, I have found somebody. But, Sir Philip, were you indulging in soliloquy?—You seem agitated.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (L.)No, sir; rather indisposed.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Upon my soul, I am devilish glad to find you. Compared with this castle, the Cretan labyrinth was intelligible; and unless some kind Ariadne gives me a clue, I shan't have the pleasure of seeing you above once a-week.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I beg your pardon, I have been an inattentive host.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Oh, no; but when a house is so devilish large, and the party so very small, they ought to keep together; for, to say the truth, though no one on earth feels a warmer regard for Robert Handy than I do—I soon get heartily sick of his company—whatever he may be to others, he's a cursed bore to me.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Where's your worthy father?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: As usual, full of contrivances that are impracticable, and improvements that are retrograde; forming, altogether, a whimsical instance of the confusion of arrangement, the delay of expedition, the incommodiousness of accommodation, and the infernal trouble of endeavouring to save it—he has now a score or two of workmen about him, and intends pulling down some apartments in the east wing of the Castle.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! ruin!—Within there!—Fly to Sir Abel Handy—Tell him to desist! order his people, on the peril of their lives, to leave the Castle instantly! Away!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Sir Philip Blandford, your conduct compels me to be serious.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Oh, forbear! forbear!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Excuse me, sir,—an alliance, it seems, is intended between our families, founded on ambition and interest. I wish it, sir, to be formed on a nobler basis, ingenuous friendship and mutual confidence. That confidence being withheld, I must here pause; for I should hesitate in calling that man father, who refuses me the name of friend.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! how shall I act?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I am not impelled by curiosity to ask your friendship. I scorn so mean a motive. Believe me, sir, the folly and levity of my character proceed merely from the effervescence of my heart—you will find its substance warm, steady, and sincere.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I believe it from my soul. Allow me a moment's thought. (aside) Suspicion is awakened; does not prudence as well as justice prompt me to confide in him? Does not my poverty command me? Perhaps, I may find a sympathizing friend — the task is dreadful — but it must be so — perhaps, he will perform the awful task of visiting the chamber, and removing every vestige of guilt. (to him)Yes, you hear my story; I will lay before your view the agony, with which this wretched bosom is loaded.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I am proud of your confidence, and, by your permission, will fulfil an important engagement awaiting me at this hour, but I speedily return to you prepared to receive it.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Not here. I will meet you at the eastern part of the castle, my young friend—mark me! this is no common trust I repose in you; for I place my life in your hands.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: And the pledge I give for its security is, what alone gives value to life, my honour.
sp.stage: Exeunt, Sir Philip, R., Bob, L.

div.head: Scene Third.— Ashfield's Cottage.
div.stage: Enter Ashfield and Dame Ashfield, R.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Come, dame. (looks off) But stay, who be thic? Why surely 'tis young Do-everything an' our Sue. Let's

div.stage: Enter Bob and Susan, L.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Do you not despise me?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (R.) No; love you more than ever.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Is it possible ?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Yes; those charms have conquered.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: Oh! no; do not so disgrace the victery you have gained—’tis your own virtue that has triumphed.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: My Susan! how true it is, that fools alone are vicious. But let us fly to my father, and obtain his consent. On recollection, that may not be quite so easy. His arrangements with Sir Philip Blandford are—are—not mine, so there’s an end of that. And Sir Philip, by misfortune, knows how to appreciate happiness. Then: poor Miss Blandford—upon my soul, I feel for her.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: (ironically) Come—don't make yourself miserable. If my suspicions he true, she’ll not break. her heart for your loss.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Nay, don’t say so—she will be unhappy.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (without, L.) Dame, shall I shoot at ‘un?

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: What does he mean ?

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: My father’s voice.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Then I'll leather ’un wi’ my stick.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Zounds! —no—come here. (calls)

div.stage: Enter Ashfield and Dame, L.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (L. C.) What do thee do here.wi’ my Sue, eh?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (R.) With your —she’s mine—mine by a husband’s right.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Husband! what! thee Sue’s husband ?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I soon shall be.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: But how though? What, faith and troth! what, like as I married dame?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: What, ax’d three times ?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p:
Yes; and from this moment I'll maintain that the real Temple of Love is a parish church—Cupid is a chubby curate, his torch is the sexton’s lantern, the
according paean of the spheres is the profound nasal thorough bass of the clerk’s amen.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Huzza! only to think now—my blessing go with you, my children !

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (L.) And mine.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: And heaven's blessing too. Ecod, I believe now, as thy feyther zays, thee canst do everything.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No; for there is one thing I cannot do—injure the innocence of woman.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Drabbit it, I shall walk in the road all day to zee Sue ride by in her own coach.

sp.speaker: Susan.
sp.p: (R. C.) You must ride with me, father.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: I say, Tummus, what will Mrs. Grundy say then?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I do hope thee will not. be asham'd of thy feyther- in-laa, woolye?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No; for then I must also be asham’d of myself, which I am resolved not to be again.

div.stage: Enter Sir Abel Handy, L.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Heydey, Bob! why an’t you gallanting your intended bride? But you are never where you ought to be.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Nay, sir, by your own confession I am where I ought to be.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No; you ought to be at the castle—Sir Philip is there, and Miss Blandford is there, and Lady Handy is there—and therefore—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: You are not there—in one word, I shall not marry Miss Blandford.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Indeed! who told you so?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: One who never lies—and therefore, one I am determined to make a friend of—-my conscience.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: But zounds, sir, what excuse have you?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (taking Susan's hand) A very fair one, sir—is not she ?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Why, yes, I can’t deny it—but, ’sdeath, sir, this overturns my best plan.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No, sir: for a parent’s best plan is his son's happiness, and that it will establish. Come, give us your consent. Consider how we admire all your wonderful inventions.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No, not my plough, Bob—but 'tis a devilish clever plough.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I dare say it is. Come, sir, consent, and perhaps, in our turn, we may invent something that may please you.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: He! he! he! well—but hold—what's the use of my consent without my wife's—bless you! I dare no more approve, without—

div.stage: Enter Gerald.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Health to this worthy company!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (C.)The same to you, sir.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: (R.)Who have we here, I wonder?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: I wish to speak with Sir Abel Handy.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I am the person.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: You are married?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Damn it! he sees it in my face.—Yes, I have that happiness.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Is it a happiness?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: To say the truth—why do you ask?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: I want answers, not questions—and depend on't 'tis your interest to answer me.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: An extraordinary fellow this!

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Would it break your heart to part with her!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Who are you, sir, that——

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Answers—I want answers—would it break your heart, I ask?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Why, not absolutely, I hope. Time, and philosophy, and——

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: I understand—what sum of money wou'd you give to the man, who would dissolve your marriage contract?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: He means something, sir.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Do you think so, Bob?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Would you give a thousand pounds?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: No!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: No; I would not give one; but I would give five thousand pounds.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Generously offered—a bargain—I'll do it.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: But, an't you deceiving me?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: What should I gain by that?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Tell me your name?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Time will tell that.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (without) Sir Abel — where are you?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: That's your wife's voice—I know it.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: So do I.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: I'll wait without—Cry, "Hem!" when you want me.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Then you need not go far— Exit Gerald, L.U.E. I dare not believe it—I should go out of my wits—and then if he fail, what a pickle I shall be in! Here she is.

div.stage: Enter Lady Handy, R.1 E.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: So, sir, I have found you at last?

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: My honoured mamma, you have just come in time to give your consent to my marriage with my sweet Susan.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: And do you imagine I will agree to such degradation?

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Do'e, Lady Nelly, do'e be kind hearted to the young loviers.—Remember how I used to let thee zit up all night a sweethearting.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Silence! and have you dared to consent?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Oh, no, my Lady!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Sir, you had better cry—"Hem."

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I think it's time, Bob—Hem!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Hem!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: What do you mean by—Hem!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Only, my dear, something troublesome I want to get rid of—Hem! Enter Gerald, L.U.E. >There he is—never was so frightened in all my life.

div.stage: (Gerald advances)

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: (shrieks and exclaims) Gerald!

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: An't you dead, Gerald? Twenty years away and not dead?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: No, wife.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Wife! did you say, wife?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Say it again.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: She is my wife.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Once more.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: My lawful, wedded wife.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Oh, my dear fellow!—Oh, my dear boy! Oh, my dear girl!—(embraces Gerald and the rest) Oh, my dear! (running to Mrs. Gerald) No—yes, now she an't my wife, I will—well—how will you have the five thousand? Will you have it in cash, or in bank notes—or stocks, or India bonds, or lands, or patents, or——

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: No—land will do—I wish to kill my own mutton.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Sir, you shall kill all the sheep in Hampshire.

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Sir Abel, you have lost five thousand pounds, and with it, properly managed, an excellent wife, who, though I cannot condescend to take again as mine—you may depend on't shall never trouble you. Come! this way (beckoning to Mrs. Gerald) Important events now call on me, and prevent my staying longer with this company. Sir Abel, we shall meet soon. Nay, come, you know I'm not used to trifle; Come, come—She reluctantly, but obediently, crosses the stage, and runs off—Gerald follows.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (imitating) Come, come—That's a wonderfully clever fellow! Joy, joy, my boy! Here, here, your hands—The first use I make of liberty, is to give happiness—I wish I had more imitators—Well, what will you do? (walks about exultingly) Where will you go? I'll go any where you like—Will you go to Bath, or Brighton, or Petersburgh, or Jerusalem, or Seringapatam? all the same to me—we single fellows—we rove about—nobody cares about us—we care for nobody.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: I must to the castle, father.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Have with you Bob. (singing) "I'll sip every flower—I'll change every hour."—(beckoning)—Come, come—
sp.stage: Exeunt Sir Abel, Bob Handy, and Susan, L. Susan kisses her hand to Ashfield and Dame.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: Bless her! how nicely she do trip it away with the gentry!

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: And then, Tummus, think of the wedding.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p:
(reflecting) I declare I shall be just the zame as
ever—may be I may buy a smartish bridle, or a zilver backy stopper, or the like o' that.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (apart) And, then, when we come out of church, Mrs. Grundy will be standing about there—

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: I shall shake hands agreeably wi' all my friends.

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (apart) Then I just look at her in this manner.

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: (apart) How dost do, Peter—Ah, Dick,—glad to zee thee wi' all my zoul.
sp.stage: (bows towards the centre of the stage)

sp.speaker: Dame.
sp.p: (apart) Then, with a kind of half curtsey, I shall—(she advances to the centre also, and they bump against each other)

sp.speaker: Ash.
sp.p: What an wold fool thee bee'st, dame—Come along, and behave pratty, do'e.

div.head: Scene Fourth.- Outside of the Castle.
div.stage: Enter Bob Handy, with caution, bearing a light, and a large key, L.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Now to fulfil my promise with Sir Philip Blandford—by—entering that chamber, and removing—'Tis rather awful—I don't half like it, somehow, every thing is so cursedly still. What's that? I thought I heard something—no—why, 'sdeath, I am not afraid—no—I'm quite su—su—sure of that—only every thing is so cursedly hush, and—(a flash of light, and a tremendous explosion takes place, R.) What the devil's that? (trembling) Who's there? Enter Sir Abel Handy, R., one side of his face is blacked.. Father!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: (trembling) Bob!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Have you seen anything!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Oh! my dear boy!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Damn it, don't frighten one—

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Such an accident! Mercy on us!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Speak!

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I was mixing the ingredients of my grand substitute for gunpowder, when somehow it blew up, and set the curtains on fire, and—

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Curtains! zounds, the room's in a blaze.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Don't say so, Bob.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: What's to be done? Where's your famous preparation for extinguishing flames?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: It is not mixed.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Where's your fire escape?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: It is not fixed.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Where's your patent fire engine?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: 'Tis on the road.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Well, you are never at a loss.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Never.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: What's to be done?

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: I don't know. I say, Bob, I have it—perhaps it will go out of itself!

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: Go out! it increases every minute—Let us run for assistance—Let us alarm the family.

sp.speaker: Sir A.
sp.p: Yes—dear me! dear me!

sp.speaker: Servant.
sp.p: (without, R.) Here, John! Thomas! some villain has set fire to the castle. If you catch the rascal, throw him into the flames.

div.stage: Sir Abel runs off, L., and the alarm bell rings, R. The effects of the fire shown on the foliage and scenery.
Enter Sir Philip Blandford, L., meeting Evergreen, R.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (L.)Emma, my child, where art thou?

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: (R.)I fear, sir, the castle will be destroyed.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: My child! my child! where is she? Speak!

sp.speaker: Ever.
sp.p: Alas! she remains in the Castle!

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! then will I die with her!

div.stage: Enter Henry, bearing Emma in his arms.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: There is your daughter.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: My child! my Emma, revive!

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: (R.)Am I restored to my dear father's arms?

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (L.)Yes, only blessing of my life! In future thy wishes shall be mine—thy happiness my joy.

div.stage: Enter Bob Handy and Susan, R.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p: My dear friend safe! and the lovely Emma in his arms! Then let the bonfire blaze.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I wish to thank you.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Sir Philip, I have saved your daughter from a dreadful death — while passing from the chamber which concealed a secret of crime and blood.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Then all is discovered?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Yes! the mystery is developed. In vain the massy bars, cemented with their cankerous rust, opposed my entrance! in vain the heated suffocating damps enveloped me! in vain the hungry flames flashed their vengeance round me! What could oppose a man struggling to know his fate? I forced the doors — a firebrand was my guide, and among many evidences of blood and guilt, I found—this! (produces a knife)

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (starts with horror, then, with solemnity) It is accomplished! Just Heaven, I bend to thy decree!—Blood must be paid by blood! Henry, that knife aimed by this fatal hand, murdered thy father!

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Ah! (grasping the knife)

sp.speaker: Miss B.
sp.p: (placing herself between him and her father) Henry! will you destroy my father?

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: Would I were in my grave!

div.stage: Enter Gerald, L.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah, Gerald here! How vain concealment! Well, come you to give evidence of my shame?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: I come to announce one, who for many years has watched each action of your life.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Who?

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: Morrington.

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: I shall then behold the man who has so long avoided me——

sp.speaker: Gerald.
sp.p: But ever has been near you—he is here.

div.stage: Enter Morrington, wrapped up in his cloak, L.

sp.speaker: Morr.
sp.p: Crawling in the dust, behold a repentant wretch!—

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: (indignantly) My brother, Morrington!

sp.speaker: Morr.
sp.p:
After the dreadful hour that parted us, agonized with remorse, I was about to finish what your arm had left unaccomplished; when some angel whispered—"Punishment is life, not death— live and atone!" I flew to you—I found you surrounded by sharpers—What was to be done?
I became Morrington! leagued with villains! practised the arts of devils! braved the assassin's steel! possessed myself of your large estates—lived hateful to myself! detested by mankind—to do what? to save an injured brother from destruction, and lay his fortune at his feet! (places parchments before Sir Philip)

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Ah! is it possible!

sp.speaker: Morr.
sp.p: Oh, is that atonement? No—But my Henry has saved that angel's life—Kneel with me, my son and beg for mercy from that injured man

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Henry, forgive me—Emma, plead for me—There—there.

sp.speaker: Henry.
sp.p: But my father——

sp.speaker: Sir P.
sp.p: Brother, I forgive thee.

sp.speaker: Bob.
sp.p:
(comes forward) If forgiveness be an attribute which ennobles our nature, may we not hope to find pardon for our errors—here?
To brighten future prospects all should now
With voice and hand unite to — Speed the Plough!

div.stage: Henry. Emma. Bob. Susan. Sir P. Morr. Gerald.
Curtain.
